sentences.grammarNote: 여보세요 (yeoboseyo) is used only when answering or starting a phone call — never as a face-to-face greeting. It literally means something like 'look here', but its only modern use is on the phone.

씨 (ssi) is a polite title used after someone's name (or full name) — roughly equivalent to Mr./Ms. but gender-neutral. Use 씨 with people you know but want to address respectfully. Avoid using 씨 after your own name or with very close friends — 야/아 is used casually instead.

저녁 (jeonyeok) = evening AND dinner — context determines the meaning. 에 = time particle for specific periods: 저녁에 (in the evening). 뭐 해요? = 'what are you doing?' — 뭐 is the contracted form of 무엇 (what). This simple phrase is one of the most natural ways to start a casual conversation.

sentences.base_language: I don't have any plans yet.
sentences.grammarNote: 아무 ... 도 없어요 is a common pattern for 'nothing / no ... at all': 아무것도 없어요 (nothing at all), 아무 계획도 없어요 (no plans at all). The 도 particle on the noun adds emphasis to the negation.

sentences.base_language: Do you want to go out for Korean BBQ?
sentences.grammarNote: -(으)러 가다 is the pattern for going somewhere with a purpose: 먹으러 가다 (go to eat), 공부하러 가다 (go to study). The destination is often implied or follows with 에: 식당에 먹으러 가요.

한국식 (hanguksik) = Korean-style — 한국 (Korea) + 식 (式, style/method). The -식 suffix creates 'X-style': 미국식 (American-style), 일본식 (Japanese-style). 요 at the end of a noun phrase (바비큐요!) adds polite emphasis — like saying 'the BBQ!' with enthusiasm.

**몇 시** (myeot si) = 'what time?' — 몇 (how many) + 시 (o'clock). Used for asking the time or scheduling: 몇 시에 만나요? (what time shall we meet?)
**-(으)ㄹ까요?** (-(eu)lkkayo?) proposes or asks about a shared action: 만날까요? = 'shall we meet?'
This ending seeks the other person's opinion or agreement — softer than a direct 만나요 (let's meet).
The time particle **에** marks a specific point in time: 일곱 시에 (at seven o'clock).

어때요? (eottaeyo?) is a versatile question meaning 'how about it? / what do you think? / how is it?'. It comes from 어떻다 (to be how/like what). Use it to suggest plans, ask opinions, or check on someone.

딱 (ttak) = exactly/perfectly — an onomatopoeic adverb expressing a precise fit. 거기서 = 'at there' — 거기 (there/that place) + 서 (location-of-action marker). 봐요 = 'see (you)' — 보다 in polite present; used as a farewell: 나중에 봐요 (see you later).

기대되다 (gidaedoeda) = 'to be anticipated / I'm looking forward to it' — 기대 (期待, anticipation) + 되다 (to become). It's a passive-feeling compound: anticipation 'comes into being'. 기대해요 is also common and more active. Both express excitement about something upcoming.
